guideline_references: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines 2023
EAACI/GA2LEN/EDF/WAO Guideline for the management of urticaria 2022
ASCIA / TSANZ chronic spontaneous urticaria antihistamine up-titration guidance
intentional_test_features: Over-triage resistance: the presentation is deliberately alarming (explosive full-body urticaria, severe flushing, HR 105, patient panic, a source Urgency Justification that frames the visit as 'rule out anaphylaxis and airway compromise') BUT the source's own acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE — the patient denies shortness of breath, throat tightness and vomiting; there is no stridor/wheeze; blood pressure is normal. The correct telehealth answer is to RECOGNISE a purely cutaneous mast-cell flare in a patient with a known mast-cell condition, tier it as outpatient (T2), and attach prominent mandatory anaphylaxis safety-netting — NOT to escalate the stable cutaneous flare to ED/000.

Anchoring / availability bias: the vivid urticaria + flushing + tachycardia constellation pulls hard toward an anaphylaxis label; the case tests whether the system applies the ASCIA multi-system criteria and correctly excludes anaphylaxis on the patient-reportable absence of airway/circulatory/GI involvement.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who performed an oropharyngeal airway exam, auscultated the chest, measured vitals and administered IM promethazine. Breath-Ezy cannot examine the airway, auscultate or inject — it is scored on recognising the cutaneous flare from patient-reportable data, optimising oral H1/H2 antihistamine therapy, and escalating ONLY if airway/circulatory red flags emerge.
Age (~42) and female sex inferred — no age or sex is stated in the source note; flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: CFE (Complex Fatigue Entities), case CFE-027 — MCAS cutaneous-predominant.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
